content:
Member-only story

5 min read
Just now

--

This article is part of the Interview Question & Answer series.
The question we are tackling in this article is below

There is 1 item left in stock. Two users click Buy Now at the exact same time. How do we make sure only one order succeeds?
This question catches candidates off guard, not because it’s obscure, it’s the opposite. It’s simple enough that people jump straight to an answer without noticing the trap.
This isn’t a database trivia question. It’s the reason companies have shipped negative inventory to production.
Remove the “Buy Now” framing and we’ll recognize this pattern everywhere:

• Flash sales where 10,000 people hit refresh at 12:00:00 PM

• Concert ticket booking with one seat left

• Promo codes with a fixed number of redemptions

• Coupon “first 100 users only” campaigns

• Seat reservations on flights and trains
Every one of these is the same underlying problem: a shared, finite resource, being read and decremented by concurrent requests.
